questions.question: What does a syndrome bit of 1 indicate?

options.text: The stabilizer commutes with the error
The stabilizer anticommutes with the error

questions.explanation: A syndrome bit of 1 means the error anticommutes with that stabilizer. When a Pauli error E anticommutes with stabilizer g, the eigenvalue changes from +1 to -1, which is recorded as syndrome bit 1.

questions.question: Why do we use ancilla qubits for syndrome measurement?

options.text: To increase the number of qubits
To measure stabilizers without collapsing the data state

questions.explanation: Ancilla qubits allow indirect measurement of stabilizer eigenvalues. The ancilla is entangled with the data through controlled operations, measured, and then reset. This extracts syndrome information without measuring the encoded data directly.

questions.question: In the syndrome measurement circuit, why is Hadamard applied to the ancilla before and after the controlled operations?

options.text: To convert between X-basis and Z-basis

questions.explanation: The first H prepares |+⟩. The controlled operations create |ψ⟩|+⟩ or |ψ⟩|−⟩ depending on the eigenvalue. The second H converts |+⟩→|0⟩ and |−⟩→|1⟩, allowing Z-basis measurement to extract the eigenvalue.

questions.question: Why doesn't syndrome measurement reveal the encoded data?

options.text: The ancillas are too weakly coupled
Stabilizers have the same eigenvalue for all code states

questions.explanation: All states in the code space are +1 eigenstates of every stabilizer. Whether the logical qubit is |0̄⟩, |1̄⟩, or any superposition, the syndrome is the same (all zeros for no error). The syndrome reveals error information, not data.

questions.question: For a code with r stabilizer generators, how many bits are in the syndrome?
questions.explanation: Each stabilizer generator gives one syndrome bit (0 or 1). With r generators, the complete syndrome is an r-bit string. For an [[n,k,d]] code, r = n-k.

questions.question: What is the syndrome for a stabilizer S (an element of the stabilizer group)?
questions.explanation: If error E is itself a stabilizer, it commutes with all stabilizer generators (since the stabilizer group is abelian). Therefore, every syndrome bit is 0. This makes sense: a stabilizer has no effect on the code space.

questions.question: Different single-qubit Pauli errors always produce different syndromes in a distance-3 code.
questions.explanation: True for correctable errors. A distance-3 code can correct any single-qubit error. This requires that each single error has a unique syndrome, allowing unambiguous identification and correction.
